title: Hand of Straights
code: static bool IsNStraightHand(int[] hand, int groupSize)
for (int card = smallest; card < smallest + groupSize; card++)
if (!count.TryGetValue(card, out int n)) return false;

frames.caption: Quick reject: hand.Length = 9, 9 % 3 = 0. Divisible, so a clean partition is still possible.
frames.note: If the card total is not a multiple of groupSize, no partition into equal-size runs can exist, so we reject in O(1) before doing any work. Here 9 % 3 = 0, so we continue.

frames.caption: Tally counts into a SortedDictionary: 1×1, 2×2, 3×2, 4×1, 6×1, 7×1, 8×1. Keys stay sorted, so the smallest remaining card is always the first key.
frames.note: The ordered count map is the engine of the greedy: it answers "what is the smallest card still in hand?" in O(log k) and lets us decrement a specific card cheaply. With k distinct cards this is O(k) space; each card is inserted once and removed once.

frames.caption: Anchor a new run at the smallest remaining card = 1. The run must be 1, 2, …, 3.
frames.note: The smallest remaining card MUST be the start of some run — nothing smaller exists to sit to its left, so no run can contain it in any later position. That makes anchoring a run at the minimum the provably safe greedy choice: committing it now can never block a better partition, because there was no other place it could go. This greedy-choice property is exactly what lets us decide locally and never backtrack.

frames.caption: card = 1: count = 1 present → consume one copy for this run.
frames.note: Having fixed the anchor, the run is forced: a straight of length groupSize must be card, card+1, …, card+groupSize−1. We decrement each consecutive count by one (removing a key when it hits zero). Because we always pulled the smallest card first, every count we touch is genuinely consumed in order — there is no ambiguity about which run a card belongs to.

frames.caption: card = 2: count = 2 present → consume one copy for this run.
frames.note: Having fixed the anchor, the run is forced: a straight of length groupSize must be card, card+1, …, card+groupSize−1. We decrement each consecutive count by one (removing a key when it hits zero). Because we always pulled the smallest card first, every count we touch is genuinely consumed in order — there is no ambiguity about which run a card belongs to.

frames.caption: card = 3: count = 2 present → consume one copy for this run.
frames.note: Having fixed the anchor, the run is forced: a straight of length groupSize must be card, card+1, …, card+groupSize−1. We decrement each consecutive count by one (removing a key when it hits zero). Because we always pulled the smallest card first, every count we touch is genuinely consumed in order — there is no ambiguity about which run a card belongs to.

frames.caption: Run #1 formed: [1, 2, 3]. Counts decremented; on to the next smallest card.
frames.note: A full straight has been carved out and its cards removed from the map. The loop now repeats on whatever the new smallest remaining card is — each iteration peels off exactly one run, so after total/groupSize iterations the map is empty.

This greedy-choice property is exactly what lets us decide locally and never backtrack.

frames.caption: count is empty: every card was consumed into a valid straight (3 runs). Return true.
frames.note: Reaching an empty map means every card fit into some consecutive run of length groupSize, anchored greedily at the smallest remaining card each time. The greedy-choice property guaranteed each local decision was safe, so no backtracking was ever needed — O(n log k) time, O(k) space.
